water_temp_read.read: Water temperature is in a favorable trout range.

light_pressure_read.read: Light and pressure do not strongly push the outlook either way.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

generated_text.quick_read: The Chattahoochee River offers a very good trout fishing outlook with ideal water temperatures around 58°F. Recent stocking enhances success using stocked trout attractors and natural nymphs.
generated_text.best_window: Day 1 morning is best because of moderate sky cover, steady light fishing pressure, and ideal water temperature, which provides comfortable feeding lanes for trout to focus on riffles, runs, seams, and structure.
generated_text.current_conditions: Water temperature is 57.9°F with a flow of 933 cfs, gage height at 2.01 ft, and turbidity at 6 NTU, indicating clear to slightly stained water conditions.
generated_text.weather_setup: Current conditions show light overcast with 21% sky cover, steady pressure at 30.05 inHg with no clear trend, mild 5.3 mph winds, and a low 13% chance of rain.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout will position near riffles, runs, seams, and structure, favoring comfortable feeding lanes in these ideal conditions. Use clean drifts with smaller natural patterns like zebra midges and pheasant tails, along with stocked trout attractors such as egg patterns, squirmy worms, and duracell nymphs. Present a balanced nymph approach with midges and soft hackles while remaining attentive to subtle fish behavior.
